coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe based on FRET for selective and sensitive detection of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhifeng
Deng, Peihong
Li, Junhua
Xu, Li
Tang, Siping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new type of fluorescent probe for detection of doxorubicin has been constructed by the combined use of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technology and molecular imprinting technique (MIT). Using doxorubicin as the template, the molecularly imprinted polymer thin layer was fabricated on the surfaces of carbon dot (CD) modified silica by sol-gel polymerization. The excitation energy of the fluorescent donor (CDs) could be transferred to the fluorescent acceptor (doxorubicin). The FRET based fluorescent probe demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity for doxorubicin. The detection limit was 13.8nM. The fluorescent probe was successfully applied for detecting doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasmas with a recovery of 96.8–103.8%, a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.3–2.8%. The strategy for construction of FRET-based molecularly imprinted materials developed in this work is very promising for analytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxorubicin
Molecular imprinting
FRET
Molecular recognition
Fluorescent carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of FRET based molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
Synthesis of CD modified silica gel (SiO2@CDs)
Preparation of molecularly imprinted material (MIM) capped silica gel (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
Determination of doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasmas
Design and synthesis of FRET based fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
Effect of pH on fluorescence emission intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIM
Fluorescence response time of SiO2@CDs@MIM for doxorubicin
Sensitivity of the prepared fluorescent probe in response to doxorubicin
Selectivity of the prepared fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
Precision and accuracy of the prepared fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of doxorubicin hydrochloride and related substrates used in this study.
UV–visible absorption spectra of doxorubicin (a) and doxorubicin in the presence of APTES (b) in water. Concentration of doxorubicin and APTES were 0.05 and 0.12mM, respectively.
Excitation and emission scans of the FRET pair, CDs and doxorubicin: excitation scan of CDs (a), emission scan of CDs (b), excitation scan of doxorubicin (c) and emission scan of doxorubicin (d).
The solid-state fluorescence emission spectra of SiO2@CDs, SiO2@CDs@NIM, SiO2@CDs@MIM and aminopropyl silica in solid state. λex =370nm.
IR spectra of aminopropyl silica (a), SiO2@CDs (b), SiO2@CDs@MIM (c) and SiO2@CDs@NIM (d).
(A) SEM micrographs of aminopropyl silica (a), SiO2@CDs@MIM (b) and SiO2@CDs@NIM(c); (B) TEM micrographs of aminopropyl silica (d), SiO2@CDs@MIM (e) and SiO2@CDs@NIM (f).
Tapping mode 3D AFM topographical images for the different surfaces studied: (a) aminopropyl silica, (b) SiO2@CDs, (c) SiO2@CDs@MIM, (d) SiO2@CDs@NIM.
XPS spectra of (a) wide scan, (b) C 1s and (c) N 1s of aminopropyl silica and SiO2@CDs.

(A) Effect of pH on the I568/I465 of SiO2@CDs@MIM in the presence of 300nM doxorubicin in 50mMPBS buffer. The pH was adjusted by adding concentrated hydrochloric acid or NaOH solution (8.0M); (B) Fluorescence response time of SiO2@CDs@MIM for doxorubicin. λex =370nm; Concentration of doxorubicin: 300nM; Concentration of the materials: 0.8mgmL−1.

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared SiO2@CDs@MIM (A) and SiO2@CDs@NIM (B) with addition of the different concentrations of doxorubicin. Concentrations of doxorubicin from bottom to top are 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 and 400nM, respectively. Concentration of the materials was 0.8mgmL−1; λex =370nm. Inset shows the linear relations between I568/I465 and the concentrations of doxorubicin from 50 to 400nM.

The selectivity of SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM for doxorubicin over other analogs including daunorubicin, bisphenol A, cholesterol and ofloxacin at concentration of 300nM. Concentration of the prepared materials was 0.8mgmL−1; λex =370nm.
Schematic representation of the preparation processes of SiO2@CDs@MIM.
Detection of doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked human plasmas.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average: M: measured by SiO2@CDs@MIM; N: measured by SiO2@CDs@NIM.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe based on FRET for selective and sensitive detection of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hengyang Normal University, Key Laboratory of Functional Organometallic Materials of Hunan Province University, Hengyang 421008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
Key Laboratory of Functional Organometallic Materials of Hunan Province University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new type of fluorescent probe for detection of doxorubicin has been constructed by the combined use of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technology and molecular imprinting technique (MIT). Using doxorubicin as the template, the molecularly imprinted polymer thin layer was fabricated on the surfaces of carbon dot (CD) modified silica by sol-gel polymerization. The excitation energy of the fluorescent donor (CDs) could be transferred to the fluorescent acceptor (doxorubicin). The FRET based fluorescent probe demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity for doxorubicin. The detection limit was 13.8nM. The fluorescent probe was successfully applied for detecting doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasmas with a recovery of 96.8–103.8%, a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.3–2.8%. The strategy for construction of FRET-based molecularly imprinted materials developed in this work is very promising for analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin is a member of anthracycline ring antibiotics with a broad spectrum of anti-tumor activity. It is one of the most effective drugs for solid tumor treatment and has gained extensive clinical application in anticancer chemotherapy [1–3]. However, application of this drug in chemotherapy must be limited because serious side-effects, such as heart damage, marrow suppression and gastrointestinal disorders, may result from its concentration beyond a certain level in the blood [4,5]. The recommended dose of doxorubicin when used as a single agent is 60–75mg·m−2 intravenously (iv) every 21days. The probability of developing cardiomyopathy is estimated to be 6–20% at a dose of 500mg·m−2, when doxorubicin is administered every 3weeks [6,7]. Wang et al. [8] reported that after iv injection of doxorubicin (50mg·m−2) 6h, the concentrations of doxorubicin in plasma of cancer patients were in the range of 0.163–0.321µM. To treat patients, it is important to prescribe an appropriate amount of a drug at a given time and concentration. Therefore, it is desirable to develop a highly sensitive and selective method for monitoring low-level doxorubicin concentrations in biological samples. Today, the most commonly used methods for doxorubicin analysis are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9–11], capillary electrochromatography and capillary electrophoresis [12–15]. However, each of these methods has its drawbacks, such as requiring expensive instrument, complicated sample preparations, and tedious operation procedures. Electrochemical method [16–18] has gained more and more attention and application in doxorubicin analysis in recent years, owing to its advantages of operational convenience, low cost and high sensitivity. However, electrochemical methods often suffer from the interference from the coexisting substances which are structurally and chemically similar to doxorubicin. Up to now, efforts have been taken to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of doxorubicin detection [19].

Molecular imprinting is an effective method for preparing artificial receptors which can bind target analytes with high selectivity [20–23]. Molecularly imprinted materials (MIMs) are suitable for selective detection of trace levels of analytes in complex samples [24–26]. Fluorescent detection is a powerful tool in chemical sensing due to its high sensitivity, simplicity and low background signal [27,28]. Fluorescent molecularly imprinted materials, which have combined the specific recognition ability of MIMs and the high sensitivity property of fluorescent detection, have drawn great attention in recent years [29–37]. Nowadays, most fluorescent sensors employ a sole responsive signal for detection, which can be readily perturbed by the change in fluorescence intensity and some external factors including environmental and instrumental efficiency. Ratiometric fluorescence detection can eliminate environmental effects and give more reliable results because it is based on the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths [31,38]. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is one of the most versatile technologies among all ratiometric fluorescence methods [39,40]. FRET occurs when an excited donor fluorophore transfers its energy to an adjacent ground-state acceptor fluorophore through dipole coupling. Through the FRET process, the donor emission is quenched and the acceptor emission is increased. The main factors which can affect FRET efficiency are: (1) the distance between donor and acceptor fluorophores, (2) the special overlap between the spectrum of donor emission and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, and (3) the desirable orientation of donor and acceptor fluorophores’ dipoles [41–44]. Considering the advantages of the FRET ratiometric fluorescence technology and the molecular imprinting technology, it is very attractive for us to design MIMs based on FRET technology for analytical applications.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nano-materials with sizes below 10nm, have found use in a wide range of fields during the last few years due to their superiority in chemical inertness, water solubility, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, etc [45–50]. Recently, CDs have also been used as fluorescent species for construction of fluorescent MIMs [34,35].

The aim of this study is to construct a molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe based on FRET for selective and sensitive detection of doxorubicin. Doxorubicin is a fluorescent cytotoxic antibiotic with emission band at about 550–650nm and excitation band at about 420–540nm. CDs were chosen as the fluorescent donor, because their emission spectrum partially overlaps with the absorption spectrum of doxorubicin and their excellent properties. The prepared fluorescent probe was well characterized and its analytical performances were investigated in detail. The FRET based fluorescent probe demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity for doxorubicin. In addition, it was successfully applied for detecting doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasmas with a recovery of 96.8–103.8% and a RSD of 1.3–2.8%.

Aminopropyl silica gel (particle size 5–25μm) was purchased from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). Bisphenol A (BPA) and cholesterol was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ofloxacin was obtained from Adamas (Shanghai, China). Glucose was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Plant (Tianjin, China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), daunorubicin hydrochloride and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) (98.5%) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Doxorubicin hydrochloride and mitoxantrone hydrochloride were obtained from Ekear (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (18.2MΩ cm) obtained from a Hitech laboratory water purification system (Hitech Instruments Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used throughout the experiments. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade. The structures of doxorubicin hydrochloride and some other compounds used in this study are shown in Fig. 1 .

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded on an IRPrestige21 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence emission was measured using a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were collected on a JEM-1230 (JEOL, Japan) microscope. A Dimension icon PT atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Swiss) was used for morphology analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on the Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging was carried out on an EVO MA10 scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV2510PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

A CTO-10AVP HPLC system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a LC-10AD pump, a RF-10AXL fluorescence detector (Shimadzu, Japan) and a C18 analytic column (250mm×4.6mm, 5µm) was used for detection of the concentrations of doxorubicin. The mobile phase was prepared with acetonitrile, methanol and water (30:50:20, v/v/v). The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 490nm and 560nm, respectively. The flow rate of the mobile phase was 1.0mL·min−1. The column temperature was set at 25°C and the sample injection volume was 10µL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FRET based molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic of the approach for the preparation of the FRET based molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe SiO2@CDs@MIM is shown in Scheme 1 . CDs were first prepared via a hydrothermal method. Then, CDs were immobilized on the surface of silica gel. Finally, the fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM) was obtained by forming a thin layer of doxorubicin-imprinted material on the surface of CD-modified silica gel (SiO2@CDs).

CDs were synthesized according to a literature method with some modifications [51]. In a typical synthesis, 5.0g of glucose was dissolved in 20mL of deionized water. After sonication for 10min, 50mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid (36.5%) was added to the solution. The mixture solution was then treated ultrasonically for 1h and transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The mixture was heated and maintained at 190°C for 24h. After the mixture was cooled naturally to room temperature, the precipitate was removed by filtration using 0.22µm filter. To remove small interfering species, the resulting solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 3days using a dialysis bag (MW: 1000). During dialysis, the deionized water was changed every 3h. The aqueous dispersion of CDs (150mL) was stored in dark at room temperature. Solid CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation and dried in vacuum at 40°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD modified silica gel (SiO2@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To a 150mL round-bottomed flask, 100mL of CD solution, 2mL aqueous solution of EDC (0.20M) and 2.0mL aqueous solution of NHS solution (0.20M) were added successively. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30min. Then, 0.50g of aminopropyl silica gel was added. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6h. The particles (SiO2@CDs) were isolated by centrifugation, washed thoroughly with water and ethanol, and dried in vacuum at 40°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of molecularly imprinted material (MIM) capped silica gel (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
ce:section.ce:para: The process of the surface imprinting onto the CD-modified silica (SiO2@CDs) is as follows: to a 150mL flask, 20mg of doxorubicin hydrochloride, 30mL of deionized water and 40µL of APTES were added. After the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30min, 80µL of TEOS (crossing linker) was injected. The mixture was kept stirring for another 5min. Then, 300mg of Si@CDs and 2.0mL of 6% NH3·H2O were added. The mixture was kept stirring for 20h before the particles were collected by centrifugation for 15min at 6000rpm. The nonimprinted material (NIM) capped silica gel (SiO2@CDs@NIM) was synthesized using the same method without addition of template molecules. The NIM-capped silica gel was washed with ethanol and deionized water successively. The synthesized SiO2@CDs@MIM was washed thoroughly with ethanol and deionized water until its fluorescence emission intensity at 465nm was similar to that of NIM-capped silica gel.

The excitation and emission slit widths were both 5nm. The excitation wavelength was set at 370nm with a recording emission range of 410–700nm. Samples were measured in the standard 10mm fluorescence quartz cuvette. SiO2@CDs@MIM or SiO2@CDs@NIM was added to the known concentration of analyte and the final concentration of particles was 0.8mgmL−1. After shaking on a rocking table for 8min, fluorescence spectra of the samples were collected at 21°C. To maintain a stable particle suspension, the samples were stirred with a built-in magnetic stirrer during the fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasmas
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM) and the nonimprinted material (SiO2@CDs@NIM) were tested against human plasmas spiked with different concentrations of doxorubicin. The doxorubicin-spiked plasmas were pre-treated as follows: 3.0mL of plasma and 3.0mL of acetonitrile were mixed in a tube. After shaking for 5min, the precipitated protein was removed by centrifugation at 6000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was transferred into another tube and evaporated under a stream of nitrogen. The residue was treated by 15.0mL of deionized water and the insoluble residues were removed by centrifugation. Then the supernatant was diluted to 50.0mL with deionized water. The samples were analyzed by SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM under the conditions as described in Section 2.3 after its pH was adjusted to a certain value by PBS buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of FRET based fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
ce:section.ce:para: The design of the molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence system is based on the fact that the FRET fluorophore pair is responsible for signal amplification and optical readout, and the molecularly imprinted materials provide specific binding sites for selective analysis. For the purpose of improving the accessibility of the specific binding sites for the target molecules and realizing fast response, surface imprinting strategy was adopted and silica gel particles were used as the supporter. In addition, construction of the fluorescent probe on the surface of silica particles can facilitate its separation and recovery from bulk solution.

The approach for preparation of SiO2@CDs@MIM is shown in Scheme 1. First, CDs were immobilized on the surface of silica gel by reacting the carboxyl groups of CDs with the amine groups of silica gel using EDC and NHS as coupling agents. Then, the CD modified silica was covered with a thin layer of doxorubicin-imprinted material through surface imprinting strategy. The thin layer of imprinted material was prepared by sol-gel polymerization using APTES as the functional monomer, TEOS as the crossing linker and doxorubicin (the acceptor fluorophore) as the template. Functional monomer plays an essential role in molecular imprinting. The presence of specific monomer–template interactions is the basis for template selective recognition sites in the resultant material. When using sol-gel polymerization approach to produce MIPs, APTES is a commonly used functional monomer because its amino group can effectively interact with various functional groups of templates. In this study, UV–visible absorption spectra were employed to study the interactions between the template and the functional monomer (Fig. 2 ). After the addition of APTES, the broad absorption band at about 481nm of doxorubicin was red-shifted by 70nm. Meanwhile, two new absorption peaks at 357nm and 592nm appeared in the spectrum. The results of UV–visible experiment demonstrated the presence of interactions between the template and the functional monomer. To achieve the thin imprinting layer (because the distance between the donor and acceptor fluorophores is an important factor which can affect FRET efficiency), the amounts of the reagents for sol-gel polymerization were carefully optimized.

CDs were easily prepared via a simple hydrothermal method using glucose as a carbon source in the presence of concentrated hydrochloric. As can be seen from Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information), under UV irradiation of 365nm, the aqueous solution of CDs presented brilliant yellow-green color. TEM image shows that the CDs exhibited as microspherical particles with an average diameter of about 3.8nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S2). The main FT-IR absorption peaks of CDs are 609, 1024, 1180, 1379, 1544, 1639, 1707, 2958, 3470 and 3650cm−1 (Supporting Information, Fig. S3). Fig. 3 shows the excitation and emission scans of the donor/acceptor pair, CDs and doxorubicin. As can be seen in Fig. 3, CDs have an emission peak at 468nm when they are excited at 370nm, while the excitation and emission peaks of doxorubicin are 498nm and 560nm, respectively. Because the emission band of CDs is largely overlapped with the absorption band of doxorubicin, energy transfer between the two fluorophores should occur when they are spatially close to each other.

The solid-state fluorescence emission spectra of SiO2@CDs, SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM were measured. As a control, the aminopropyl silica gel was also measured under the same conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , SiO2@CDs displayed a maximum fluorescence emission at 465nm when excited at 370nm, while aminopropyl silica gel displayed almost no fluorescence emission. The results can verify the successful grafting of CDs onto silica gel. It is worthy to note that the fluorescence intensities of SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM are weaker than those of SiO2@CDs. This fluorescence decreasing might be ascribed to the fact that a part of the emissive light has been adsorbed when it passed through the thin silica shell [34].

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized materials. Compared with aminopropyl silica, the FT-IR spectrum of SiO2@CDs demonstrates the characteristic amide I absorption band at 1641cm−1 and amide II band at 1558cm−1, suggesting the existence of amide bonding (–CO–NH–). Besides, the absorption band at 3000–3600cm−1 increased obviously. This is due to the fact that there are abundant carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups in CDs [51]. The FT-IR spectrum of SiO2@CDs@MIM is similar to that of SiO2@CDs, except that the absorption bands at 2850–2960cm−1 (C–H stretching band) and 3000–3600cm−1 (the stretching vibration of −OH and −NH2) are further increased. This is due to the increasing of the aminopropyl group content in SiO2@CDs@MIM. The FT-IR spectra of SiO2@CDs@NIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM are quite similar.

SEM and TEM were used to morphologically characterize the particles (Fig. 6 ). The SEM images of the aminopropyl silica show a homogenous and smooth surface morphology. Compared to the aminopropyl silica particles, surface morphologies of SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM particles indicate an improving surface roughness. From Fig.6B, we can see that after the sol-gel reaction, the surfaces of SiO2@CDs particles were covered with a thin incompact solid layer.

AFM images of aminopropyl silica, SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM are shown in Fig. 7 . Fig.7a reveals that the surface of aminopropyl silica is quite smooth (with Ra roughness of 2.1nm), which is consistent with the SEM result. Fig.7b shows that the surface of SiO2@CDs sample is obviously rougher (with Ra roughness of 5.3nm) than that of the aminopropyl silica sample. This is caused by immobilization of CDs on the surface of aminopropyl silica. As can be seen from Fig.7c and d, after the surface molecular imprinting process, the surface roughness of the particles (with Ra roughness of 10.1nm for SiO2@CDs@MIM and 11.2nm for SiO2@CDs@NIM) is further increased.

XPS spectra can provide valid information of the elements in the surface layer. The overview spectrum obtained on the SiO2@CDs surface in Fig.8 a shows a strong increase in C 1s and O 1s intensities in comparison to the spectrum measured on the aminopropyl silica surface. After being modified by CDs, the contents of C, O, and N elements (Atomic %) changed from 9.01%, 53.31%, and 2.80% to 14.01%, 61.52% and 2.35%, respectively.

Fig.8b and c shows the high-resolution C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra measured on the aminopropyl silica surface and SiO2@CDs surface. The C 1s signals of aminopropyl silica at 285.1 and 286.2eV binding energies are attributed to aliphatic C (C–C/C–H) and nitrogen-bonded C (C-N), respectively. The C 1s signal from the SiO2@CDs can be fitted with five peaks with binding energies at 285.0, 286.2, 287.5, 288.4, and 289.2eV, corresponding to aliphatic C, nitrogen-bonded C (C-N), oxygen-bonded C (C−O), carbonyl C in amide (CONH) and carboxy C (COOH), respectively [52–56]. The N 1s scan of aminopropyl silica in Fig.8c shows a peak at 399.7eV binding energy confirming the presence of amino N (NH2). In the case of SiO2@CDs, a new peak at 401.3eV binding energy appeared. We assigned it to the signal of cationic ammonium group N. The results of XPS measurements further confirmed that CDs have been successfully introduced to the surface of aminopropyl silica.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence emission intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIM
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission of SiO2@CDs@MIM response to doxorubicin was investigated. It was found that when increasing amounts of doxorubicin were added into the solution, the CD emission at 465nm gradually decreased, and the doxorubicin emission band at 568nm increased, indicating that the energy of the donor CDs could be transferred to doxorubicin. The influence of pH value on the fluorescence emission of SiO2@CDs@MIM response to doxorubicin was investigated, using the ratio of the fluorescence emission intensities at 568nm and 465nm, defined as I568/I465, as the index. As can be seen in Fig.9 A, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I568/I465) of SiO2@CDs@MIM is strongly influenced by the pH value. Enhanced values of I568/I465 were obtained in the pH 6–9 range. When the pH is lower than 6, the nitrogen atom of amino group in the recognition site of SiO2@CDs@MIM can bind a proton to form a cation, which decreased the interaction of SiO2@CDs@MIM and doxorubicin. When the pH was higher than 9, the phenolic hydroxyl group of doxorubicin will be deprotonated, which can also decrease the interaction between doxorubicin and the imprinted cavities. Experimental results showed that the maximum value of I568/I465 was observed at the pH of 7, thus this pH value was selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response time of SiO2@CDs@MIM for doxorubicin
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the accessibility of SiO2@CDs@MIM for doxorubicin, we investigated the response time of the fabricated fluorescent probe. After different incubation times, the fluorescence emission intensities at 568nm of SiO2@CDs@MIM-doxorubicin mixtures have been measured (Fig.9B). The figure shows that the emission intensity at 568nm increased fast within 8min, after which the curve became flat. The constant emission intensity value signified an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Therefore, 8min was chosen as the response time for further experiments. The response time of the material is quite short, which can be attributed to the fact that the imprinting layer is very thin. The thin imprinting layer on the surface of silica demonstrated advantages of low mass transfer resistance and high site accessibility toward the template doxorubicin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the prepared fluorescent probe in response to doxorubicin
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of the prepared fluorescent probe for doxorubicin, the fluorescence spectra of SiO2@CDs@MIM in the presence of different amounts of doxorubicin were collected (Fig.10 A). A working curve was established by plotting the ratio of the emission intensities at 568nm and 465nm (I568/I465) versus doxorubicin concentration (inset of Fig.10A). As seen from the figure, the ratio of the intensities at 568nm to that at 465nm (I568/I465) increased steadily with doxorubicin concentration increasing. The calibration was linear in the concentration range within 50–400nM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 13.8nM according to the 3σ/S criteria, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration. Compared with the reported methods in the literature, including capillary electrophoresis [13], HPLC [9,10], electrochemical analysis [16], etc., the LOD of the proposed method in the present work is comparable or in the same order of magnitude.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence property of the non-imprinted material (SiO2@CDs@NIM) to doxorubicin was also examined. From Fig.10B, we can see that the fluorescence intensities at both 465nm and 568nm bands have changed slightly with the addition of doxorubicin, and hence, the sensitivity was much lower than that of SiO2@CDs@MIM (inset of Fig.10B). For the SiO2@CDs@MIM, there are molecularly imprinted sites on the surface of silica matrix, so doxorubicin can enter the recognition sites easily, and thus quenching the fluorescence intensity of the donor CDs obviously and increasing the fluorescence intensity of doxorubicin. As for SiO2@CDs@NIM, there are no imprinted sites on the surface of silica matrix. Doxorubicin can only be adsorbed by SiO2@CDs@NIM via non-specific interaction. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the donor (CDs) in SiO2@CDs@NIM can only be quenched by non-specific adsorption of doxorubicin. The low binding of doxorubicin caused a slight variation of the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the prepared fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions, daunorubicin, bisphenol A, cholesterol and ofloxacin (Fig. 1) were selected as the analog target compounds for the template to investigate the selective recognition ability of SiO2@CDs@MIM. It can be seen from Fig. 11 that doxorubicin induced a prominent fluorescence ratio (I568/I465) increment compared to the blank sample (no compound was added). Besides doxorubicin, daunorubicin also caused an obviously fluorescence ratio (I568/I465) increment. In contrast, addition of other compounds led to almost no fluorescence ratio change. As we know, the fluorescence response of the prepared molecularly imprinted probe was based on FRET. The substrate which can induce a fluorescence ratio change must meet the following two basic requirements: (1) The structure of the substrate is complementary in both shape and functional group arrangement to the recognition site; (2) The absorption spectrum of the substrate partially (or entirely) overlaps the emission spectrum of CDs for FRET. Fig. 1 shows that the structure of daunorubicin is similar to that of doxorubicin. Thus it can enter the recognition site produced by doxorubicin imprinting. In addition, daunorubicin is a fluorescent compound and its emission band is at about 550–650nm when excited at 430–520nm, which meets the requirement of FRET. Therefore, daunorubicin has induced an obviously fluorescence ratio increment. Other selected compounds, i.e., bisphenol A, cholesterol and ofloxacin do not meet the two basic requirements mentioned above and therefore they cannot induce a fluorescence response. The results implied that the FRET based MIM had a higher selectivity for the template than ordinary MIMs due to the double recognition abilities, i.e., structure recognition and fluorescent recognition.

The selectivity of SiO2@CDs@MIM for doxorubicin was also checked in the presence of another anthracycline drug (i.e. mitoxantrone). To carry out the competition absorption experiment, 800nM of doxorubicin solution was mixed with an equal volume of 800nM of mitoxantrone solution. Since the volume ratio of these two solutions is 1:1, the final concentration of doxorubicin, as well as mitoxantrone, was 400 nM in the mixture solution. The mixture solution was analyzed by SiO2@CDs@MIM under the conditions as described in Section 2.3. For the mixture solution, the fluorescence ratio (I568/I465) was 2.12, which is very close to the I568/I465 value (2.15) obtained from the 400nM of doxorubicin solution. The result indicates that the fluorescence response of SiO2@CDs@MIM could not be affected by the coexisting analogs The chemical structure of mitoxantrone is different from the template in terms of size and shape. Thus, it is difficult for mitoxantrone to fit into the cavity produced by doxorubicin imprinting. The results of competitive adsorption experiment further confirmed that the fluorescent probe has high molecular recognition ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precision and accuracy of the prepared fluorescent probe (SiO2@CDs@MIM)
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of the prepared fluorescent probe, it was employed to analyze human plasma samples spiked with different amounts of doxorubicin. Method validation was performed in terms of accuracy and intra- and inter-day precision (RSD %). The accuracy of the method was determined by calculating the recovery of doxorubicin by the method of standard additions. Precision was calculated as the relative standard deviation of the determined concentration. For repeatability and intermediate precision studies of the method, five replicate determinations on the same day and on five different days of analytes (blood plasmas spiked at concentrations of 50, 200 and 400nM) were carried out. The recoveries and the RSDs of SiO2@CDs@MIM and SiO2@CDs@NIM were calculated and are summarized in Table 1 . In the case of SiO2@CDs@MIM, the recoveries were in the range of 96.8–103.8% while the RSDs were found to be 1.3–2.1% for the run-to-run precision and 1.5–2.8% for the day-to-day precision. The results obtained based on SiO2@CDs@MIM were in agreement with those obtained by the HPLC. However, very low recoveries (14.3–43.0%) were obtained when SiO2@CDs@NIM were used. This could be easily explained because there are no imprinted sites on the surface of SiO2@CDs@NIM and thus, doxorubicin can only be adsorbed by non-specific interaction. The results achieved in this study indicated that the prepared fluorescent probe can be applied to the detection of doxorubicin in real samples.

In this work, fluorescent probe based on FRET for selective and sensitive detection of doxorubicin was prepared. The fluorescent probe was fabricated by surface molecular imprinting technique using doxorubicin as the template, APTES as the functional monomer, TEOS as the crossing linker, and CD modified silica as the supporter. The fluorescent probe demonstrated high levels of sensitivity and specificity for doxorubicin. Satisfactory precision and accuracy were obtained when using the fluorescent probe for determination of doxorubicin in doxorubicin-spiked plasma samples. Considering the high sensitivity property of ratiometric fluorescent detection, the high selectivity originating from the double recognition requirements, the approach of constructing FRET-based fluorescent probe developed in this work is very promising for analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [21472038 and 21505035], the Project of Education Department of Hunan Province [15A027], the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province [2016JJ3028], the Aid Programs for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province and the Key Discipline of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2017.02.005.
